verses.esv: In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth.
insights: barāʾ (ברא) is never used with a human subject anywhere in the Hebrew Bible. People build, form, and make — but only God barāʾ.
ʾelohim (אלהים) is plural in form — literally 'Gods' — yet the verb barāʾ is singular. The first sentence holds this tension without resolving it.
Genesis 1:1 has exactly 7 words and 28 letters — 7×4. The same number as the days of creation that follow.

verses.esv: The earth was without form and void, and darkness was over the face of the deep. And the Spirit of God was hovering over the face of the waters.
insights: tohu vavohu (תהו ובהו) — formless and void — rhymes in Hebrew. The sound itself feels empty and chaotic. No translation can carry that rhythm.
ruaḥ (רוח) — usually translated Spirit — also means wind and breath. All three meanings are alive simultaneously in this verse. Translation must choose one. Hebrew holds all three.
meraḥefet (מרחפת) — hovering — is feminine in form, agreeing with ruaḥ which is also feminine. The Spirit hovering over the waters is grammatically feminine throughout.

verses.esv: And God said, "Let there be light," and there was light.
insights: wayomer (ויאמר) — and he said — is the first time speech appears in the Bible. God's first act after creating is speaking. The Hebrew makes this sequence unmistakable.
yehi ʾor (יהי אור) — let there be light — is only three words in Hebrew. The shortest divine command in Scripture. The brevity itself feels intentional.
ʾor (אור) appears twice in this verse — once commanded, once fulfilled. The same word echoes back immediately, like a question answered by itself.

verses.esv: And God saw that the light was good. And God separated the light from the darkness.
insights: ṭov (טוב) — good — appears here for the first time in the Bible. It will appear 7 times across Genesis 1. Again the number 7, woven into the fabric of the text.

wayavdel (ויבדל) — separated — comes from the root b-d-l (בדל). The same root appears in Leviticus for the separation of clean and unclean. Separation is a deeply loaded concept throughout Hebrew scripture.
wayyarʾ ʾelohim (וירא אלהים) — God saw — uses the same verb as ordinary human sight. No elevated divine vocabulary. Just — he saw.

verses.esv: God called the light Day, and the darkness he called Night. And there was evening and there was morning, the first day.
insights: wayiqraʾ (ויקרא) — he called — naming in Hebrew is an act of authority. When God names something he is establishing its nature. The same verb is used when Adam names the animals.

ʿerev (ערב) and boqer (בקר) — evening and morning — appear in this order throughout Genesis 1. The Hebrew day begins at sundown not sunrise. Every Jewish Sabbath still begins Friday evening because of this verse.
yom ʾeḥad (יום אחד) — day one — uses ʾeḥad meaning one, not riʾshon meaning first. All other days use ordinal numbers. Only day one is different. Scholars have debated why for centuries.
